metadata.title: Introducing Theoristai
metadata.color_semantics: claude fidelity: INK #3D3929; SPARK #D97757 one accent.
metadata.author: Nik Bear Brown
metadata.credit_line: Nik Bear Brown — Founder, Humanitarians AI
metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Nik Bear Brown — Founder, Humanitarians AI. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Theorist.ai reframes AI as the opposite of obsolescence for human intelligence. A forklift extends rather than replaces the human who operates it. Gardner's 1983 multiple-intelligences framework was built before machines could truly compete. It never asked which intelligences technology endangered, because then none were threatened.

props.artifactTitle: Rewriting Gardner for the AI age
artifactLines: Theorist.ai reframes AI as the opposite of obsolescence for human intelligence.
A forklift extends rather than replaces the human who operates it.
Gardner's 1983 multiple-intelligences framework was built before machines could truly compete.
It never asked which intelligences technology endangered, because then none were threatened.

beats.narration_text: The inversion that AI threatens not obsolescence but its opposite, and that Gardner's 1983 multiple-intelligences framework never had to ask which intelligences were endangered because technology wasn't yet a competitor, reframes the whole stakes for me. Help me: (1) list which of Gardner's intelligences now face a capable machine rival; (2) explain the forklift analogy's claim that the tool does not make the human obsolete; (3) sketch what a Theorist.ai reframing of Gardner for the AI era would add.

props.command: The inversion that AI threatens not obsolescence but its opposite, and that Gardner's 1983 multiple-intelligences framework never had to ask which intelligences were endangered because technology wasn't yet a competitor, reframes the whole stakes for me. Help me: (1) list which of Gardner's intelligences now face a capable machine rival; (2) explain the forklift analogy's claim that the tool does not make the human obsolete; (3) sketch what a Theorist.ai reframing of Gardner for the AI era would add.
output: There is a specific kind of student I have watched for ten years
Not because the problem is too hard
This is the educational catastrophe hiding in plain sight

beats.narration_text: Not obsolescence — its opposite. That’s why I started Theorist.ai.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Not obsolescence — its opposite. That’s why I started Theorist.ai.

beats.narration_text: The forklift does not make the human obsolete. That was the right argument for its moment.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The forklift does not make the human obsolete. That was the right argument for its moment.

beats.narration_text: Gardner’s framework was built before machines became capable. It did not need to ask which intelligences were endangered by technology, because technology — in 1983 — was not yet a serious competitor to any of them.

beats.narration_text: Written by Nik Bear Brown — Founder, Humanitarians AI. Humanitarians AI is an educational bridge program where Fellows learn experiential AI by doing AI — building real projects and shipping real research.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Paste this into Claude — Take the central idea from 'Introducing Theoristai' and give me the three most actionable takeaways — one sentence each. Then… That's the prompt that actually tests whether you got it. Run it and see where it surprises you.

props.command: Take the central idea from 'Introducing Theoristai' and give me the three most actionable takeaways — one sentence each. Then name the failure mode I'm most likely to hit.
